questions.question: What is the doctrine of privity of contract?

options.A: The rule that parties who have agreed to keep the terms of their contract private may do so
options.B: The rule that only the parties to a contract can sue upon or be bound by it — a third party cannot acquire rights or be subjected to obligations under a contract to which they are not a party
options.C: The rule that contracts must be formed in private, without witnesses
options.D: The equitable doctrine that prevents unconscionable enforcement of contracts

questions.explanation: The doctrine of privity provides that a contract creates rights and obligations only between the parties to it. A third party who was not a party to the contract — even if the contract was made for their benefit — cannot sue on it, nor can they be bound by obligations imposed on them by it. This doctrine was affirmed in Dunlop Pneumatic Tyre Co v Selfridge & Co [1915] (HL) and remains the foundation of the law in Ireland.

questions.question: The doctrine of privity was classically established in Tweddle v Atkinson [1861]. What were the facts and holding of that case?

options.A: A third party beneficiary could sue because the contract was expressly for his benefit
options.B: The son (intended beneficiary) could not sue because he was not a party to the agreement between his father and father-in-law, and had provided no consideration
options.C: The courts enforced the third party's right because the contract was made in contemplation of his marriage
options.D: The son was entitled to sue because equity recognised his beneficial interest

questions.explanation: In Tweddle v Atkinson [1861], the plaintiff's father and his intended father-in-law agreed that each would pay a sum to the plaintiff on his marriage. The father-in-law died without paying. The plaintiff sued on the agreement. The court held he could not succeed: he was not a party to the agreement and had provided no consideration. This case classically stated the requirement that consideration must move from the promisee.

questions.question: In Dunlop Pneumatic Tyre Co v Selfridge & Co [1915], the House of Lords confirmed the privity rule. What was the contractual chain at issue?

options.A: Dunlop sold directly to Selfridge, who then resold to the public at below list price
options.B: Dunlop sold to Dew (a dealer), who agreed not to sell below list price; Dew sold to Selfridge, who then undercut the list price; Dunlop sued Selfridge despite being a stranger to the Dew-Selfridge contract
options.C: Dunlop and Selfridge were parties to the same contract but Selfridge repudiated it
options.D: Dunlop assigned its contractual rights to a third party who then sued Selfridge

questions.explanation: In Dunlop v Selfridge [1915], Dunlop sold tyres to Dew on condition that Dew would not resell below list price. Dew sold to Selfridge, who agreed to observe the price restriction. Selfridge undercut the price. Dunlop sued Selfridge directly, but failed — Dunlop was a stranger to the Dew-Selfridge contract and could not enforce a term of it. The privity doctrine barred the claim.

questions.question: What is the current position in Irish law regarding the right of a third party to enforce a contract made for their benefit?

options.A: Ireland has enacted legislation (similar to the UK Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999) giving third parties a direct right to enforce
options.B: Ireland has not enacted third-party rights legislation — the common law doctrine of privity applies with equitable and common law exceptions
options.C: Irish courts freely enforce third-party contractual rights by analogy with trust principles
options.D: The Irish Constitution guarantees third-party rights to enforce contracts made for their benefit

questions.explanation: Unlike England and Wales (which enacted the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999), Ireland has not enacted equivalent legislation. The doctrine of privity applies in full. However, Irish courts have developed and applied exceptions including agency, trusts of promises, assignment, and specific statutory exceptions. The Law Reform Commission has recommended reform, but as of 2025–26 no legislation has been enacted.

questions.question: Which of the following is an established common law or equitable exception to the privity rule under Irish law?

options.A: Where a contract is made orally between two parties in the presence of a third party
options.B: Where one contracting party acts as agent for a third party who is named or identifiable as principal
options.C: Where the third party would suffer loss if the contract were not performed
options.D: Where the subject matter of the contract is a consumer good

questions.explanation: Agency is a well-established exception to privity. Where one party contracts as agent for an identified or identifiable principal, the principal (third party) can sue and be sued on the contract as if they were a party to it. The agent drops out of the contract (or is an additional party) once the principal is disclosed. The undisclosed principal doctrine also allows a principal to intervene on certain contracts made by the agent in their own name.

questions.question: In Drimmie v Davis [1899] (Ireland), the courts applied the 'trust of a promise' exception to privity. This exception operates where:

options.A: A contract is assigned from one party to a third party with the other contracting party's consent
options.B: A promisee holds the benefit of a contractual promise on trust for a third party, who may then enforce the promise in equity
options.C: A deed is executed conferring a benefit on a third party
options.D: A statutory provision makes the contract enforceable by a third party

questions.explanation: The trust of a promise exception allows a third party to enforce a contract where the promisee holds the benefit of the promise on trust for the third party. The third party can sue in equity, joining the promisee as a defendant if necessary. In Drimmie v Davis [1899], an Irish court recognised a covenant in a partnership deed as enforceable by a third-party beneficiary through trust principles. The courts have been restrictive in finding a trust, however, requiring clear intention to create a trust.

questions.question: In The Eurymedon [1975] (New Zealand Shipping Co v Satterthwaite & Co) (PC), the Privy Council found a way to avoid the privity rule by holding that:

options.A: A stevedore (third party) could rely on an exclusion clause in the bill of lading through agency — the carrier contracted as agent for the stevedore
options.B: The privity rule did not apply to commercial contracts
options.C: The stevedore had an implied contractual right to benefit from all terms in the bill of lading
options.D: The stevedore's tort liability was excluded by the Hague Rules

questions.explanation: In The Eurymedon [1975], the Privy Council held that a stevedore (third-party contractor) could rely on the exception clause in the bill of lading. The carrier had contracted as agent for the stevedore; by unloading the goods, the stevedore provided consideration for the shipper's promise. A collateral contract was identified — the shipper offered to exempt the stevedore from liability, and the stevedore accepted by performing. This creatively used agency and collateral contract to circumvent privity.

questions.question: Which of the following represents a statutory exception to the privity rule in Irish law?

options.A: The Consumer Rights Act 2022 giving consumers rights against manufacturers
options.B: Third-party motor insurance under the Road Traffic Acts — an injured third party has a direct right against the insurer
options.C: The Companies Act 2014 allowing shareholders to sue for company debts
options.D: The Statute of Frauds 1695 creating enforceable obligations on third parties

questions.explanation: The Road Traffic Acts (Ireland) create a statutory exception to privity in motor insurance. A third party who suffers injury or damage from a road traffic accident has a direct right of action against the wrongdoer's insurer, despite not being a party to the insurance contract. This statutory exception reflects the public policy objective of ensuring accident victims can recover compensation.

questions.question: In Hearn v Collins [1998] (HC Ireland), the court considered whether a third party could enforce an agreement between two solicitors. The case illustrates which principle?

options.A: Solicitors' agreements between themselves are binding on their clients as a matter of professional conduct
options.B: A third party who is the intended beneficiary of an inter-solicitor undertaking may have a direct cause of action, the court considering whether trust or agency principles apply
options.C: Inter-solicitor agreements are binding only in the context of litigation
options.D: Solicitors cannot enter binding contracts on behalf of clients without express authority

questions.explanation: In Hearn v Collins [1998], the Irish High Court considered whether a third-party beneficiary (the client of one solicitor) could enforce an undertaking given by another solicitor. The case engaged with the exceptions to privity, including trust of promise and the equitable basis for direct enforcement of solicitors' undertakings. Irish courts have been attentive to the policy of allowing beneficiaries to enforce professional undertakings made for their benefit.

questions.question: What is meant by 'collateral contract' as a device to overcome privity?

options.A: A second contract made as security for the first
options.B: A separate contract between a party to the main contract and a third party, under which the third party provides consideration (e.g., entering the main contract) in exchange for a promise from the contracting party
options.C: A contract that runs alongside the main agreement but is unenforceable
options.D: A standard form contract imposed by one party on another

questions.explanation: A collateral contract is a separate contract between one party to the main contract and a third party. The third party's consideration is usually entering into (or performing) the main contract. This device allows a third party to enforce a promise that would otherwise fail for privity. In Shanklin Pier v Detel Products [1951], the pier owner was able to enforce a promise by a paint manufacturer directly because they had entered the main painting contract in exchange for the manufacturer's promise about the paint.
